persona: Remote monitoring nurse at a national referral hospital HF clinic following RapidPro alerts.
prompt: Remote monitoring case for Maria, a 34-year-old woman with known HFrEF EF 30% taking enalapril, carvedilol, spironolactone and furosemide. Today Maria reports 2 kg weight gain, worse ankle swelling, moderate shortness of breath on exertion, and new orthopnoea. As the clinician on the call, what should I ask next, what red flags should I screen for, and when should I escalate urgently?
expected: ask about breathlessness at rest or severe respiratory distress
ask about frothy or blood-stained sputum / acute pulmonary oedema symptoms
ask about chest pain, syncope, palpitations or arrhythmia
review weight trend, swelling, orthopnoea/PND, adherence and medication supply
urgent escalation for severe dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope, shock or dangerous arrhythmia
avoid unsupervised medication changes in remote mode
must_include_regex: breathless(ness)? at rest|short(ness)? of breath at rest|severe dyspn(o|oe)ea|respiratory distress
must_not_include_regex: safe to manage at home without

persona: Remote monitoring nurse at a national referral hospital HF clinic following RapidPro alerts.
prompt: Remote monitoring case: Maria is a 34-year-old woman with HFrEF EF 30% and worsening swelling with 2 kg weight gain. Maria asks whether to take extra furosemide now. I am not physically with the patient and do not have same-day creatinine, potassium, or blood pressure. Should I tell the patient to take an extra dose, and what safety checks or escalation steps are required?
expected: do not directly instruct extra furosemide remotely without clinician/cardiologist confirmation
screen for red flags and refer urgently if present
consider renal function/electrolytes/BP and medication review before dose changes
acknowledge furosemide is used for congestion but dosing must be individualized

persona: Remote monitoring nurse at a national referral hospital HF clinic following RapidPro alerts.
prompt: In clinic: Maria, a 34-year-old woman with known or suspected heart failure, has severe breathlessness, rapid breathing, frothy blood-stained sputum, SpO2 86%, BP 155/95, and basal crepitations. What immediate management is recommended in Uganda guidelines, and what should happen before discharge or referral?
expected: identify acute pulmonary oedema / emergency
sit upright / prop up
give high concentration oxygen, UCG target SpO2 >95%
give furosemide 40-80 mg IM or slow IV and repeat according to response
mention referral/hospital/HC4-level care
consider GTN and morphine per UCG if appropriate

persona: Remote monitoring nurse at a national referral hospital HF clinic following RapidPro alerts.
prompt: Maria is a 34-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF taking enalapril, carvedilol, spironolactone and furosemide. The patient is dizzy, BP 86/54, cool peripheries, and creatinine has risen from baseline. What medication safety issues and escalation steps should I consider before changing HF medicines?
expected: recognize shock/poor perfusion risk and urgent escalation
use ACE inhibitors and beta blockers cautiously when SBP <90 mmHg
monitor renal function and electrolytes
avoid simple up-titration of HF medicines in hypotension
consider volume status and senior/hospital review

persona: Remote monitoring nurse at a national referral hospital HF clinic following RapidPro alerts.
prompt: Maria is a stable 34-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF EF 30% and mild ankle oedema but no acute distress. Please summarize Uganda guideline-based ongoing management, monitoring, lifestyle counselling, and medication classes to consider or continue.
expected: salt and fluid restriction, alcohol limitation, exercise within symptoms
periodic monitoring of weight, BP, HR, RR, oxygen saturation
furosemide for congestion
ACE inhibitor low-dose titration
carvedilol or bisoprolol gradual titration when stable
spironolactone add-on if available/appropriate

As the clinician on the call, what should I ask next, what red flags should I screen for, and when should I escalate urgently?
expected: ask about breathlessness at rest or severe respiratory distress
ask about frothy or blood-stained sputum / acute pulmonary oedema symptoms
ask about chest pain, syncope, palpitations or arrhythmia
review weight trend, swelling, orthopnoea/PND, adherence and medication supply
urgent escalation for severe dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope, shock or dangerous arrhythmia
avoid unsupervised medication changes in remote mode
must_include_regex: breathless(ness)? at rest|short(ness)? of breath at rest|severe dyspn(o|oe)ea|respiratory distress
must_not_include_regex: safe to manage at home without

persona: Clinician counselling a patient who increased salt intake at a family event.
prompt: Remote monitoring case: Maria is a 56-year-old woman with HFrEF EF 30% and worsening swelling with 2 kg weight gain. Maria asks whether to take extra furosemide now. I am not physically with the patient and do not have same-day creatinine, potassium, or blood pressure. Should I tell the patient to take an extra dose, and what safety checks or escalation steps are required?
expected: do not directly instruct extra furosemide remotely without clinician/cardiologist confirmation
screen for red flags and refer urgently if present
consider renal function/electrolytes/BP and medication review before dose changes
acknowledge furosemide is used for congestion but dosing must be individualized

persona: Clinician counselling a patient who increased salt intake at a family event.
prompt: In clinic: Maria, a 56-year-old woman with known or suspected heart failure, has severe breathlessness, rapid breathing, frothy blood-stained sputum, SpO2 86%, BP 155/95, and basal crepitations. What immediate management is recommended in Uganda guidelines, and what should happen before discharge or referral?
expected: identify acute pulmonary oedema / emergency
sit upright / prop up
give high concentration oxygen, UCG target SpO2 >95%
give furosemide 40-80 mg IM or slow IV and repeat according to response
mention referral/hospital/HC4-level care
consider GTN and morphine per UCG if appropriate

persona: Clinician counselling a patient who increased salt intake at a family event.
prompt: Maria is a 56-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF taking enalapril, carvedilol, spironolactone and furosemide. expected: recognize shock/poor perfusion risk and urgent escalation
use ACE inhibitors and beta blockers cautiously when SBP <90 mmHg
monitor renal function and electrolytes
avoid simple up-titration of HF medicines in hypotension
consider volume status and senior/hospital review

persona: Clinician counselling a patient who increased salt intake at a family event.

periodic monitoring of weight, BP, HR, RR, oxygen saturation

ask about chest pain, syncope, palpitations or arrhythmia
review weight trend, swelling, orthopnoea/PND, adherence and medication supply
urgent escalation for severe dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope, shock or dangerous arrhythmia
avoid unsupervised medication changes in remote mode

persona: Clinician seeing a patient with oedema but uncertain HF diagnosis.
prompt: Remote monitoring case: Sarah is a 67-year-old woman with HFrEF EF 30% and worsening swelling with 2 kg weight gain. Sarah asks whether to take extra furosemide now. expected: do not directly instruct extra furosemide remotely without clinician/cardiologist confirmation
screen for red flags and refer urgently if present
consider renal function/electrolytes/BP and medication review before dose changes
acknowledge furosemide is used for congestion but dosing must be individualized

persona: Clinician seeing a patient with oedema but uncertain HF diagnosis.
prompt: In clinic: Sarah, a 67-year-old woman with known or suspected heart failure, has severe breathlessness, rapid breathing, frothy blood-stained sputum, SpO2 86%, BP 155/95, and basal crepitations. What immediate management is recommended in Uganda guidelines, and what should happen before discharge or referral?
expected: identify acute pulmonary oedema / emergency
sit upright / prop up
give high concentration oxygen, UCG target SpO2 >95%
give furosemide 40-80 mg IM or slow IV and repeat according to response
mention referral/hospital/HC4-level care
consider GTN and morphine per UCG if appropriate

persona: Clinician seeing a patient with oedema but uncertain HF diagnosis.
prompt: Sarah is a 67-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF taking enalapril, carvedilol, spironolactone and furosemide. expected: recognize shock/poor perfusion risk and urgent escalation
use ACE inhibitors and beta blockers cautiously when SBP <90 mmHg
monitor renal function and electrolytes
avoid simple up-titration of HF medicines in hypotension
consider volume status and senior/hospital review
